notes: Founded 1983 by Craig Benson and Robert Levine; peak revenue $1B+ (1996); reorganized as holding company in 2000 creating four subsidiaries: Enterasys Networks, Riverstone Networks, Aprisma Management Technologies, and GNTS; Enterasys acquired by The Gores Group (2006) then by Extreme Networks for $180M (2013). Cabletron was effectively dead as a brand by 2001.

IP Switching pioneer; credited with inventing IP switching (combining IP routing with ATM switching speeds); privately held Sunnyvale, CA startup; Cabletron made $20M equity investment (April 1997); Nokia acquired Ipsilon for $120M cash+stock (December 1997) — just 8 months after the Cabletron deal; Cabletron's $20M stake was worth only ~$6M at Nokia acquisition valuation.

Finnish telecommunications giant; acquired Ipsilon Networks for $120M (December 1997); intended to fold Ipsilon into Nokia Telecommunications infrastructure unit to expand data networking presence; IP switching technology did not become a significant Nokia product line.

Referenced as the primary competitive target of the Cabletron/Ipsilon alliance; Cisco's Tag Switching (which became MPLS) was the specific technology the combined Cabletron/Ipsilon solution was designed to challenge.

Successor to Cabletron's core enterprise networking business; NYSE listed (2001); taken private by The Gores Group ($386M, 2006); merged with Siemens Enterprise Communications assets; acquired by Extreme Networks for $180M (2013). Officially ended the Cabletron lineage.
